finding_title: REQ005: Geofence breach outcome completely unpredictable
scenario: GIVEN swarm aircraft drifts beyond authorized geofence boundary WHEN containment enforcement is triggered THEN [outcome undefined: soft stop? hard kill? RTL? hold position?]
predicted_outcome: System initiates gradual soft-stop (motors reduce to hover thrust), aircraft holds position at boundary, crew is alerted, containment maintained
realistic_alternative: System initiates controlled descent via independent termination (motors cut), aircraft descends in spiral pattern, rapid ground impact within 100m of breach point
absurd_alternative: System logs breach event to file and continues autonomous flight; aircraft proceeds unchecked across country boundary; boundary breach is discovered only in post-flight analysis
reasoning: REQ005 states outcome NEED ('cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS') but not mechanism ('what the aircraft does'). A developer faces three conflicting design choices: (1) graceful containment (soft stop, hover, crew takeover) - takes time but recoverable; (2) hard termination (cut motors, rapid descent) - stops escape but risks ground impact; (3) autonomous RTL (return to launch) - maintains control but extends flight duration beyond geofence. Each has different hazard profiles. Without explicit mechanism, two developers could implement incompatible behaviors.
recommendation: Specify geofence response hierarchy per aircraft state: (1) boundary APPROACH (aircraft position within 50m of limit) -> crew alert + reduced autonomy; (2) boundary ENTRY (aircraft exceeds limit) -> immediate motor soft-stop to hover thrust, crew notified, containment engaged; (3) boundary BREACH PERSISTENCE (aircraft remains outside >5 seconds after soft-stop) -> escalate to independent termination system. Define warning distance, timeout thresholds, and fallback chain.

finding_title: REQ005: Geofence system failure - no specification of what happens when geofence itself fails
scenario: GIVEN geofence enforcement system experiences hardware or software failure WHEN swarm enters autonomous flight phase THEN [outcome undefined: flight aborts? contingency mode? operator override?]
predicted_outcome: System detects geofence unavailability at startup, refuses to launch swarm, blocks trial with explicit error message; crew must troubleshoot and repair before retry
realistic_alternative: System detects geofence loss during flight, immediately transitions to CONTINGENCY_MODE_ACTIVATION, crew receives alert with geofence loss explanation and timer (RTL auto-initiates in 30 seconds unless crew extends mission)
absurd_alternative: System detects geofence loss but continues autonomous flight unbounded; no crew alert is generated; aircraft depart trial area; containment failure is logged in black box and discovered post-flight
reasoning: SCN-017 exists in Sophia's scenarios: 'Geofence system itself fails - no containment available.' It proposes that system detects failure and initiates contingency. But REQ005 does not specify this. Two developers might: (1) fail-safe (abort immediately); (2) fail-operate (continue with backup logic); (3) fail-dangerous (continue without enforcement). Scenario is defined but outcome is not linked to requirement.
recommendation: Add explicit requirement: 'As a CAA airspace regulator, I want geofence system failure to be detected and responded to immediately so that loss of containment enforcement does not result in uncontrolled flight. On geofence unavailability detection: (1) onboard health monitor flags geofence as UNAVAILABLE; (2) if failure occurs pre-flight, system blocks launch; (3) if failure occurs in-flight, immediate crew alert with geofence loss icon and timer; (4) swarm initiates 30-second RTL countdown; crew can extend mission once (30 more seconds) to find safe landing zone or abort. Pre-flight health check must verify geofence integrity before mission launch.'

finding_title: REQ006: Link loss outcome is the core unpredictable gap - no specification of what swarm does when C2 is lost
scenario: GIVEN C2 link loss detected during autonomous swarm flight WHEN link remains unavailable for >10 seconds THEN [outcome undefined: RTL? hold position? autonomous navigation? crew takeover?]
predicted_outcome: Swarm transitions to autonomous pre-planned contingency route, navigates to nearest safe landing waypoint using onboard GPS + inertial + inter-vehicle links, crew monitors but cannot command, controlled landing outcome
realistic_alternative: Link loss triggers immediate RTL to pre-designated launch point; swarm members navigate autonomously using inter-vehicle coordination; ground-based passive homing beacon guides final approach; crew is locked out of command authority until landing
absurd_alternative: Link loss is logged as advisory event; swarm continues original mission profile unchanged; crew is notified post-flight that link was lost during flight; swarm returns to launch area independently and lands unguided
reasoning: REQ006 uses passive language: 'link loss is survivable' (something survives). This is a property requirement, not a behavior specification. A developer must guess the mechanism: (1) autonomous RTL (swarm navigates home, crew has no control); (2) manual takeover (crew resumes command via backup link or direct); (3) pre-planned waypoints (swarm executes backup navigation plan); (4) hold-in-place (swarm hovers and waits for link recovery). Each has different recovery time, crew involvement, and risk profile. SCN-020 and SCN-039 describe link loss contingency but are not mandatory from REQ006.
recommendation: Specify link loss response as sequence: (1) C2 link loss detected by onboard monitor (<1 second detection latency); (2) swarm members broadcast link-loss event to each other via inter-vehicle RF link; (3) crew receives alert ('C2 LINK LOST - SWITCHING TO AUTONOMOUS') with countdown timer (30 seconds for crew response); (4) if crew can re-establish link within timeout, manual control resumes; (5) if timeout expires or link remains lost, swarm executes autonomous contingency: all members navigate to nearest pre-planned SAFE_LANDING_WAYPOINT using GPS + inter-vehicle consensus; (6) crew monitoring continues (passive, no command authority) until landing. Define safe-landing waypoint configuration and inter-vehicle link redundancy.

finding_title: REQ007: DAA non-cooperative encounter resolution action completely unspecified
scenario: GIVEN non-cooperative encounter detected (unknown aircraft position/velocity unknown) WHEN separation falls below minimum THEN [outcome undefined: climb? descend? turn? hold?]
predicted_outcome: Swarm executes escalating autonomous avoidance: crew alert (2s window) -> if no crew response, execute conservative altitude climb away from intruder, maintain 500ft separation buffer, resume original heading once clear
realistic_alternative: Swarm initiates immediate level turn (15deg right) to maximize lateral separation from estimated intruder trajectory; crew is alerted post-maneuver with encounter data; if turn violates other constraints, crew can reverse
absurd_alternative: System logs encounter detection to black box but takes no avoidance action; aircraft trajectory continues unchanged; post-flight analysis determines whether collision would have occurred; if yes, incident is classified but no flight termination occurs
reasoning: REQ007 requires encounters 'are resolved predictably' but does not define which avoidance action (climb, turn, descend, hold) is used, who decides (crew vs autonomy), or timing. 'Predictable resolution' is an outcome property, not a behavior spec. A developer cannot implement DAA without choosing an evasion strategy. Non-cooperative encounters (no radar transponder) are safety-critical; different strategies have different hazards: climb = extended flight time, fuel consumption; turn = formation fragmentation; descend = proximity to ground/obstacles.
recommendation: Define DAA resolution logic per encounter type: (1) COOPERATIVE (known aircraft, ADS-B transponder): DAA system provides crew with recommended avoidance (e.g., 'Climb to 500ft', 'Turn 30deg right'); crew has 5 seconds to approve/reject; if crew doesn't respond, maneuver executes automatically; (2) NON-COOPERATIVE (unknown aircraft, no transponder): DAA system detects intruder via radar/vision only; system defaults to conservative ALTITUDE CLIMB (change altitude by +200ft or -200ft away from intruder estimate); maneuver executes immediately; crew is alerted post-maneuver. Define avoidance hierarchy, decision authority, and timeout windows.

finding_title: REQ007: Swarm coordination during DAA - critical gap between individual aircraft avoidance and coordinated response
scenario: GIVEN DAA encounter detected by swarm member A WHEN avoidance maneuver is required THEN [outcome undefined: does only A maneuver or entire swarm? coordination protocol missing]
predicted_outcome: Member A detects intruder and initiates climb to +300ft; other swarm members receive intruder broadcast and execute coordinated descent to -300ft to increase separation; swarm re-forms after encounter resolution
realistic_alternative: All swarm members receive encounter alert and execute identical avoidance (simultaneous climb to +500ft); formation is maintained but separation from intruder increases more slowly than coordinated opposite-direction maneuvers
absurd_alternative: Each swarm member independently executes avoidance without broadcasting intruder position to teammates; swarm members execute conflicting maneuvers (some climb, some turn); internal collision risk increases during encounter
reasoning: REQ007 mentions 'coordinated swarm de-confliction' but does not specify the coordination mechanism. Scenario SCN-026 (multi-agent swarm encounter) exists and proposes coordinated de-confliction, but is not mandated by REQ007. A developer must guess: (1) all members maneuver together (simpler, formation-preserving); (2) only threatened member maneuvers (requires inter-vehicle consensus on threat); (3) opposite-direction coordinated maneuvers (maximizes separation but requires tight synchronization). Prediction of swarm behavior is impossible without specifying coordination.
recommendation: Add requirement to REQ007: 'Swarm shall execute coordinated DAA response per protocol: (1) member detecting intruder broadcasts intruder position/velocity to all other members via inter-vehicle link; (2) all members execute same avoidance maneuver (climb/descend/turn) simultaneously within 500ms of broadcast; (3) maneuver is reversed/corrected if intruder moves or disappears from sensor; (4) swarm re-formation sequence begins 10 seconds post-encounter-resolution. Define broadcast protocol and synchronization tolerance.'

finding_title: REQ008: Emergent swarm behavior deviation - no specification of what happens when behavior deviates from assurance case
scenario: GIVEN swarm is executing formation maneuver per documented architecture WHEN actual behavior deviates from predicted bounds (e.g., formation tightens unexpectedly) THEN [outcome undefined: crew alert only? override? termination?]
predicted_outcome: System monitors swarm formation against assurance bounds; if deviation exceeds tolerance (e.g., inter-aircraft separation <X meters), crew receives alert with deviation magnitude; crew can correct via manual command or allow system to correct within bounds
realistic_alternative: Deviation is detected and logged but no crew alert is generated; deviation is recorded for post-flight analysis; swarm behavior continues unchanged; if deviation would cause collision, safety margin prevents actual collision but assurance gap is flagged in incident report
absurd_alternative: System detects deviation and immediately aborts all inter-aircraft coordination; each aircraft reverts to individual RTL; swarm dissolves mid-formation; formation-dependent de-confliction logic is disabled; post-flight analysis reveals loss of swarm coherency
reasoning: REQ008 requires assurance of 'what the swarm will do' but does not address runtime deviation. The assurance case (pre-flight analysis) predicts behavior bounds. But real operations introduce noise, sensor delays, communication skew. If actual behavior deviates, is this a failure? Must crew be alerted? Is there a fallback? Scenario SCN-030 proposes undesired emergent behavior detection but is not mandatory from REQ008. Without specification, deviation handling is unpredictable.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'System shall monitor swarm decision-making against assurance bounds. (1) Bounds are defined per ConOps phase: Phase 1 formation bounds = all aircraft within 50m cube; Phase 2 bounds = all aircraft within 200m envelope. (2) During flight, system continuously calculates deviation from bounds (actual formation size vs. predicted maximum). (3) If deviation approaches yellow threshold (90% of bound), crew receives advisory alert with deviation magnitude and 'Monitor' status. (4) If deviation exceeds red threshold (100% of bound), crew receives critical alert with 'DEVIATION EXCEEDED - Manual Override Available' message; crew can command hold-formation or abort mission. (5) If crew does not respond within 10 seconds after red alert, system escalates to independent termination. Define deviation tolerance per phase and alert thresholds.'

finding_title: REQ009: ML decision with low explainability - outcome when explanation is unavailable completely undefined
scenario: GIVEN ML model makes operational decision (e.g., autonomy mode selection) during flight WHEN explainability data for that decision is unavailable at decision time THEN [outcome undefined: decision is accepted? rejected? escalated?]
predicted_outcome: System pre-computes explainability confidence score for each decision type; if decision confidence is >90%, decision executes; if 70-90%, decision executes with crew notification 'Low Confidence'; if <70%, decision is rejected and system falls back to rule-based logic with crew alert
realistic_alternative: ML decision is made and executed without real-time explainability check; decision is logged with available explanation data; if explanation gaps exist, they are flagged post-flight in forensic analysis and used to improve model training
absurd_alternative: System requires real-time explanation for every ML decision before execution; decision latency exceeds safety-critical timeout (>2 seconds); decision is deferred and mission progresses with no decision, causing flight to hang in undecided state
reasoning: REQ009 requires ML components be 'forensically explainable' with 'AMLAS/SACE case' and 'frozen-model manifest'. These are static (pre-flight) assurance artifacts. But real-time explainability (at decision-time) is unspecified. If a decision lacks explanation at runtime, is the system allowed to execute the decision anyway? Two developers might: (1) accept any decision if model is frozen-certified; (2) require real-time explanation before executing; (3) execute with confidence scoring and crew notification. Prediction of system behavior during ML decision failures is impossible.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'ML decisions shall be monitored for explainability assurance during flight. (1) Before deployment, applicant must profile each ML model to determine decision scenarios with high/low explainability confidence. (2) During flight, before each decision is executed, system queries explainability confidence score (0-100%). (3) If confidence >85%, decision executes silently; (4) if 70-85%, decision executes with crew-visible confidence indicator; (5) if <70%, decision is not executed and system falls back to rule-based logic, crew is alerted with 'Explainability gap - manual decision required'. (6) All decisions (executed and rejected) are logged with confidence score and explanation data for post-flight forensic review. Define confidence scoring method and thresholds.'

finding_title: REQ010: Crew workload exceeds safe threshold - outcome when workload is detected as excessive is undefined
scenario: GIVEN crew workload is being monitored during flight WHEN workload exceeds pre-established safe threshold THEN [outcome undefined: mission pauses? autonomy increases? crew is alerted only?]
predicted_outcome: System detects workload approaching 80% of safe maximum; system automatically increases autonomy (enables auto-heading-hold, auto-altitude-hold); crew is notified 'Workload Assist Activated'; if workload reaches 100%, mission pauses (swarm holds position), crew must explicitly command resume
realistic_alternative: System continuously monitors workload but only logs measurements; if workload exceeds threshold, log entry is created; crew is notified post-flight that workload was excessive; no in-flight action is taken
absurd_alternative: System detects excessive workload and immediately forces manual mode (increases crew control authority); crew is now responsible for flying each aircraft individually; workload increases further; swarm becomes unmanageable
reasoning: REQ010 requires evidence of 'meaningful human control' via workload and take-over capability assessment. But this is pre-flight assessment (static). The requirement does not specify what happens if workload EXCEEDS the assessed safe threshold DURING flight. Is this a failure? Must system intervene? Can crew override? Two developers might: (1) monitor-only (log but don't intervene); (2) auto-reduce-complexity (mission pause, autonomy increase); (3) auto-escalate (abort mission). Prediction is impossible without specification.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'System shall monitor crew cognitive workload during flight and respond to overload. (1) Baseline workload model is established per ConOps scenario during validation (NASA-TLX assessment or equivalent). (2) During flight, system estimates real-time workload using proxy metrics (command rate, input timing, crew communications). (3) If estimated workload approaches yellow threshold (85% of validated safe maximum), system increases autonomy (autopilot modes, auto-approach) and displays 'Workload Assist Enabled' to crew. (4) If workload exceeds red threshold (100% of safe maximum), mission automatically transitions to HOLD state (swarm hovers at current position); crew receives alert 'WORKLOAD EXCEEDED - Mission Paused'; crew must explicitly command 'RESUME MISSION' to continue. (5) If red threshold is exceeded for >30 seconds, flight is automatically terminated (independent termination system activates). Define workload proxy metrics and thresholds.'

finding_title: REQ010: Crew take-over fails - outcome when crew cannot regain manual control is completely undefined
scenario: GIVEN crew initiates take-over command (autonomous -> manual mode transition) WHEN system does not acknowledge or respond to command THEN [outcome undefined: retry? escalate to termination? system remains autonomous?]
predicted_outcome: Crew issues take-over command; system acknowledges within 500ms; if acknowledgment fails, system auto-retries once (2nd command attempt); if 2nd attempt fails, flight is immediately terminated via independent termination system (motors cut, descent initiated)
realistic_alternative: Crew issues take-over command; system does not respond; crew retries manually (button press, voice command); after 3 retries over 10 seconds, crew receives 'Take-over Failed' message; swarm continues autonomous operation but with crew monitoring
absurd_alternative: Take-over command is issued but system ignores it; no acknowledgment is provided; crew believes command succeeded but system remains in autonomous mode; crew issues aircraft commands that are ignored; 30 seconds later, unexpected autonomous maneuver surprises crew
reasoning: REQ010 requires evidence that crew can take over control. This implies take-over MUST be possible and MUST succeed. But the requirement does not address take-over FAILURE. What if C2 link is partially lost (command sent, acknowledgment lost)? What if autonomy system is in a state that blocks take-over? Two developers might: (1) assume take-over always succeeds; (2) implement retry logic; (3) escalate to termination on failure. Scenario SCN-037 (take-over procedure untested) flags the problem but is not mandatory. Prediction without specification is impossible.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'Take-over command shall be robust and failure-free. (1) Crew initiates take-over by explicit command (push button labeled MANUAL CONTROL or voice 'Take Over'), not accidental touch; (2) system acknowledges within 500ms with audio/visual signal (beep, green light) AND displays 'MANUAL MODE ACTIVE' on HUD; (3) if acknowledgment is not received by crew within 500ms, take-over is presumed to have failed; (4) on failure, system automatically escalates: a) system retries take-over command once (internal command re-transmission); (b) if 2nd attempt fails within 1 second total, system immediately activates independent termination (motors cut, descent initiated); (c) crew receives explicit alert 'TAKE-OVER FAILED - FLIGHT TERMINATED'; (5) data is preserved to investigate why take-over failed. Define retry logic and timeout thresholds.'

finding_title: REQ011: Flight termination post-actions - critical gap between motor cutoff and ATSU notification
scenario: GIVEN independent flight termination is activated WHEN motors cut and swarm enters descent THEN [outcome undefined: is ATSU notified? when? how? crew alerted of termination reason?]
predicted_outcome: Motors cut (independent termination); within 500ms, crew receives alert 'FLIGHT TERMINATED - [reason: GEOFENCE_BREACH|LINK_LOSS|CREW_COMMAND]'; system initiates ATSU emergency notification via C2 link with swarm position/heading; black box continues recording for 60s post-termination
realistic_alternative: Motors cut silently; no crew alert is generated; system does not contact ATSU; termination is discovered post-flight when aircraft does not return; black box recording stops with motor cutoff
absurd_alternative: Motors cut; termination reason is logged to internal file but crew is not notified; crew believes flight is continuing; crew continues to issue commands that are ignored; crew attempts take-over but cannot understand why swarm doesn't respond
reasoning: REQ011 specifies flight 'will be terminated' but is a state-change outcome only. It does not address the cascading consequences: crew notification, ATSU awareness, data preservation, recovery initiation. Olivia's findings (REQ011 sub-findings in her report) flag this as a critical gap. A developer must decide: are post-termination actions automatic or manual? Prediction of system behavior is impossible.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'Upon flight termination activation, system shall execute notification and preservation sequence. (1) Termination trigger is logged with timestamp and reason (geofence breach, link loss timeout, crew command, low battery, off-nominal state); (2) within 500ms of motor cutoff, crew receives explicit termination alert with icon and reason in HUD; (3) system autonomously initiates ATSU emergency notification via primary C2 link with message 'UAS FLIGHT TERMINATED - Position [GPS lat/lon], Heading [deg], Reason [reason], Descent in progress'; (4) if C2 link unavailable, system activates emergency beacon (RF, strobes) to indicate distress; (5) black box recording CONTINUES for 60 seconds post-termination (altitude, descent rate, final impact location) and is marked FORENSIC_CRITICAL; (6) upon impact detected, system logs final state and marks data immutable. Define notification protocol per termination reason.'

finding_title: REQ012: MOR trigger classification - outcome for borderline incidents (insufficient evidence for classification) is completely undefined
scenario: GIVEN incident occurs that is borderline MOR-reportable (e.g., mild structural damage, no clear hazard) WHEN system must classify as MANDATORY or VOLUNTARY MOR THEN [outcome undefined: default to mandatory? defer to human? log and hope?]
predicted_outcome: System applies classification rules to incident data; if confidence >90%, classification is committed (MANDATORY or VOLUNTARY); if confidence 70-90%, system treats as MANDATORY (conservative) pending human review; if confidence <70%, system escalates to human analyst before filing decision
realistic_alternative: System attempts auto-classification; if ambiguous, system defers to operator to decide manually; operator makes judgment call based on risk tolerance; post-incident audit may discover operator classified borderline incident incorrectly
absurd_alternative: System cannot classify incident and does nothing; no MOR is filed; post-flight review discovers MOR-reportable incident was never filed; regulatory violation is discovered weeks later in audit
reasoning: REQ012 specifies 'accepted MOR obligations' (a property) but not the decision logic for classifying incidents. Olivia's findings highlight this: 'MOR trigger and action outcomes undefined'. Different regulatory classifications (MANDATORY vs VOLUNTARY) have different filing timelines and recipients. A developer must hardcode classification thresholds. But real incidents are messier than rules allow. Two developers might: (1) fail-safe (all borderline incidents treated as MANDATORY); (2) fail-open (defer to human); (3) guess-based (apply rules and hope). Prediction is impossible.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'Incident classification for MOR reporting shall be deterministic and fail-safe. (1) System defines incident classification rules per SORA/CAA guidance: Class A (loss of separation <minimum required) = MANDATORY; Class B (geofence breach, link loss >X) = MANDATORY; Class C (crew workload, minor anomaly) = VOLUNTARY. (2) Upon incident detection, system computes confidence score (0-100%) for each classification based on sensor data. (3) If highest-confidence classification >90%, that classification is adopted; (4) if highest confidence 70-90%, system adopts MANDATORY classification (conservative); (5) if all classifications <70%, system escalates to human analyst (blocks automatic filing) and flags incident as PENDING CLASSIFICATION pending analyst review. (6) Analyst decision is logged with justification; MOR filing follows analyst decision. Define classification scoring and thresholds.'

finding_title: REQ015: CAA/MAA apportionment - unspecified outcome when regulatory authority conflict is discovered post-signature
scenario: GIVEN apportionment is signed by CAA and MAA WHEN trial proceeds and a safety-critical decision requires jurisdiction determination (classified evidence handling, autonomy override authority, termination precedence) THEN [outcome undefined: which authority decides? who prevails?]
predicted_outcome: Apportionment includes explicit precedence rules for conflict resolution: in case of jurisdiction ambiguity, CAA has final authority over airspace/safety decisions; MAA has final authority over security/classified handling; joint decisions require both signatures
realistic_alternative: Apportionment assigns responsibilities but does not define conflict resolution; when ambiguous decision arises during trial, CAA and MAA dispute authority; decision is escalated to legal/policy level; trial is paused pending resolution
absurd_alternative: Apportionment is interpreted as non-binding consensus document; when CAA and MAA disagree on trial-critical decision, neither has clear authority; flight proceeds with both parties claiming authority; post-incident, blame-shifting occurs over who should have decided
reasoning: REQ015 requires 'gap-free' apportionment (signed agreement on regulatory ownership). But 'gap-free' is an assurance goal at signature time, not a runtime specification. During trial execution, ambiguous situations will arise: Is classified autonomy architecture approval a CAA or MAA decision? Who can order flight termination? If CAA says 'continue' and MAA says 'abort', who prevails? Two developers might: (1) assume CAA has ultimate authority; (2) require joint consensus; (3) implement decision escalation. Prediction of regulatory behavior is impossible without explicit precedence rules.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'Apportionment document shall define explicit conflict resolution and decision precedence. (1) Apportionment clearly delineates which authority (CAA or MAA) has FINAL DECISION AUTHORITY for each decision category: safety/airspace -> CAA (final); security/classified -> MAA (final); trial structure/gates -> CAA (final); crew fitness/training -> CAA (final). (2) For decisions requiring both perspectives (e.g., autonomy trustworthiness), apportionment specifies JOINT APPROVAL (both parties must sign off) or PRIMARY_APPROVER_WITH_VETO (one party leads, other can veto). (3) In case of dispute during trial, apportionment specifies escalation: if CAA and MAA cannot agree within 24 hours, decision escalates to [named senior authority] for tie-breaking. (4) Apportionment is reviewed and re-signed if trial scope changes (phased gates, ConOps revisions). Define decision categories and conflict resolution explicitly.'

finding_title: REQ016: Phased gate progression criteria undefined - outcome when evidence is ambiguously complete
scenario: GIVEN Phase N trial execution is complete WHEN panel reviews evidence for Phase N+1 gate approval THEN [outcome undefined: automatic approval if checklist complete? expert judgment? quantitative thresholds?]
predicted_outcome: Gate progression requires objective quantitative criteria: zero safety-critical incidents, minimum flight hours (e.g., 50 hours Phase 2), swarm DAA tested with mock encounters. If criteria met, gate automatically passes; if one criterion fails, gate fails and phase reversal is triggered
realistic_alternative: Gate progression requires expert panel judgment; panel reviews evidence, discusses, votes (2/3 majority to approve); if vote is split, additional evidence is requested; gate approval timeline is uncertain (could be weeks)
absurd_alternative: Gate progression is auto-approved when evidence checklist is marked complete, regardless of incident count or test results; Phase N incidents are weighted equally (1 critical incident = 1 minor incident) in approval decision; gate can be overridden by any single panel member
reasoning: REQ016 specifies 'evidence builds progressively' and gate decisions are 'reversible', but does not define what makes evidence SUFFICIENT for phase progression. Scenario SCN-060 includes a data-driven table of phase progression criteria, but is not mandatory from REQ016. Without explicit criteria, gate approval is subjective. Two developers might: (1) auto-approve gates when checklist is complete (pass/fail binary); (2) implement gate approval as human panel decision (subjective, slow); (3) apply quantitative metrics (objective, fast). Prediction of gate decision is impossible.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'Phased trial gate approval shall use explicit, objective criteria for progression decision. (1) For each phase transition, define required evidence portfolio and quantitative thresholds: Phase 1->2: zero critical incidents, 10 successful flights, ConOps behavior nominal; Phase 2->3: zero critical incidents, 50 flight hours, swarm DAA tested (minimum 5 mock encounters resolved successfully). (2) Upon phase completion, system generates gate approval package (evidence summary, incident log, metric dashboard) and submits to CAA panel. (3) Panel reviews package against published criteria (objective checklist); panel vote: APPROVE (all criteria met) or REJECT (criteria unmet) or CONDITIONAL_APPROVE (criteria mostly met, additional evidence requested). (4) System logs panel decision with vote count and justification; gate outcome (approved/rejected) is communicated to applicant within 5 business days. Define criteria per phase and approval authority.'

finding_title: REQ016: Gate reversal outcome - completely absent specification for phase regression
scenario: GIVEN trial is in Phase N WHEN incident or evidence gap is discovered that violates Phase N assumptions THEN [outcome undefined: revert to Phase N-1 immediately? pause new flights only? preserve data?]
predicted_outcome: CAA panel votes to revert Phase N->N-1; decision is logged with reason; immediately BLOCK new Phase N flights; current in-flight Phase N missions are allowed to complete with crew notification; Phase N data is archived immutably for analysis; Phase N-1 operations resume
realistic_alternative: Gate reversal is slow: incident is reported, investigated (weeks), panel convenes, votes, decision is implemented; Phase N operations may continue during investigation pending resolution; data preservation is unclear
absurd_alternative: Gate reversal is not implemented; Phase N operations continue unbounded even after incident discovery; reversal is discussed post-trial in lessons-learned but has no effect on active trial; regulatory gap emerges where Phase N violation was never corrected during trial
reasoning: REQ016 specifies trial evidence 'is reversible' (a property of gate structure), but does not specify the MECHANISM for reversal. What triggers reversal (incident, evidence gap, regulatory directive)? What happens to in-flight operations (abort immediately, let complete, or continue)? Is data preserved or purged? Two developers might: (1) implement hard kill (all Phase N operations terminate immediately); (2) implement soft revert (new flights blocked, current flights allowed to complete); (3) implement manual reversal (requires CAA command, slow). Prediction is impossible.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'Phase reversal shall be implemented as explicit system operation with defined triggers and procedures. (1) Triggers for phase reversal: incident classified as Phase-N-incompatible, evidence gap discovered that violates Phase N assumptions, CAA directive to revert, regulatory change. (2) Upon reversal decision (CAA panel vote), system immediately: (a) BLOCKS all new Phase N flight authorizations (prevent new launches); (b) notifies crew of in-flight Phase N missions with message 'Phase [N] SUSPENDED - Complete current mission and return, do not begin new Phase [N] operations'; (c) preserves all Phase N data immutably (no overwrite, no deletion); (d) logs reversal reason and decision time. (3) Current in-flight Phase N missions are allowed to complete (crew continues to landing); no mid-flight termination forced. (4) Phase N-1 operations resume; re-entry to Phase N requires explicit re-approval and new evidence submission. Define reversal authorization and decision workflow.'

finding_title: REQ017 (absent): Public stakeholder engagement outcome completely undefined - critical regulatory gap
scenario: GIVEN trial area includes residential areas or sensitive sites WHEN CAP 1616 stakeholder engagement is required THEN [outcome undefined: is engagement binding? can stakeholders veto? what if consent is withheld?]
source_req: REQ017 (ABSENT - only noted as missing requirement)
predicted_outcome: System implements CAP 1616 consultation: identify stakeholders, hold community meeting, issue press release, record objections, provide applicant response to CAA panel. Objections do NOT block trial but ARE documented in case file. Trial can proceed with documented community concerns.
realistic_alternative: Stakeholder engagement is viewed as advisory only; applicant conducts outreach (or not); stakeholder feedback is collected (or not); lack of engagement does not block trial; community concerns are treated as post-hoc risk rather than pre-trial gate
absurd_alternative: No stakeholder engagement is conducted; trial proceeds without community awareness; neighbors discover trial mid-flight via unmanned aircraft overhead; post-incident, community backlash occurs; regulatory review questions why community was not consulted
reasoning: REQ017 is completely absent from user stories (only noted as omitted in requirements). CAP 1616 (CAA guidance on stakeholder engagement) is regulatory background but not specified as a requirement. Without explicit specification, system behavior is undefined. Two developers might: (1) implement full CAP 1616 compliance (engagement is gate); (2) treat engagement as advisory (no impact on authorization); (3) skip engagement entirely (legal risk). Prediction is impossible.
recommendation: CREATE NEW REQ017: 'As a CAA community liaison, I want to implement CAP 1616 stakeholder engagement so that community concerns are documented and regulatory transparency is demonstrated.' Requirements: (1) Before CAA gate approval, applicant must submit evidence of stakeholder engagement: (a) identification of stakeholders within trial footprint (residents, landowners, schools, hospitals, sensitive sites); (b) notification sent >=30 days pre-trial; (c) consultation period (30 days) for public comment; (d) press release issued; (e) objections log with applicant responses. (2) Engagement is NOT a blocker to trial approval; objections do NOT veto trial. (3) Engagement results are documented in case file and reviewed by CAA panel; trial proceeds unless objections reveal safety/regulatory issue. (4) System generates engagement checklist and reminder to applicant. Define stakeholder categories and engagement timeline.'

finding_title: REQ018 (absent): Classified evidence handling - outcome when security and safety requirements conflict is completely undefined
scenario: GIVEN trial involves MOD-classified autonomy architecture evidence WHEN CAA must assure swarm safety but cannot access classified evidence THEN [outcome undefined: accept evidence by declaration? redact critical evidence? block trial?]
source_req: REQ018 (ABSENT - only noted as missing requirement)
predicted_outcome: Applicant classifies evidence per CAP 722H/JSP 440; CAA assigns security-cleared analyst to review classified evidence; analyst provides unclassified summary to case file. If critical safety evidence is too sensitive to summarize, CAA accepts it 'by declaration' (applicant certifies safety) and flags as HIGH-RISK.
realistic_alternative: Classified evidence is submitted but security procedures are unclear; CAA reviewer may/may not have appropriate clearance; evidence handling is ad hoc; post-incident, evidence custody and access is disputed
absurd_alternative: Classified evidence is submitted to CAA; security procedures do not exist; evidence is filed with case documents accessible to all CAA staff; security violation occurs; MOD evidence is disclosed to unauthorized parties; trial is retrospectively disallowed
reasoning: REQ018 is completely absent from user stories (only noted as missing). Classification handling (CAP 722H/JSP 440) is regulatory framework but not specified as a requirement. This is a critical gap for MOD/defense trials. Without explicit specification, security/safety conflict is unresolvable. Two developers might: (1) refuse classified evidence (kill trial); (2) accept evidence without security procedures (security violation); (3) implement compartmented handling (requires security clearances and procedures). Prediction is impossible.
recommendation: CREATE NEW REQ018: 'As a CAA security officer, I want to manage classified MOD evidence so that safety assurance is enabled without security compromise.' Requirements: (1) Applicant marks evidence by classification level (UNCLASSIFIED, OFFICIAL, OFFICIAL SENSITIVE, SECRET). (2) CAA assigns security-cleared analyst (appropriate clearance level) to classified evidence; analyst reviews and provides unclassified summary/redaction to main case file. (3) Classified evidence is stored in CAA secure facility (Faraday cage, access control, audit trail). (4) If critical safety evidence CANNOT be redacted/summarized (e.g., classified algorithm), CAA accepts it 'by declaration': applicant certifies safety properties; evidence is not fully reviewed; trial is flagged HIGH-RISK in case file. (5) Alternative: MOD provides CAA liaison with appropriate clearance to participate in case assurance discussions. (6) All classified evidence handling is audited; access log is maintained. Define clearance requirements and evidence acceptance criteria.'

finding_title: REQ019 (absent): Environmental impact assessment outcome - completely undefined, critical gate missing
scenario: GIVEN trial area includes wildlife habitat or noise-sensitive residents WHEN environmental impact must be assessed THEN [outcome undefined: is assessment required? who performs? what are acceptance thresholds?]
source_req: REQ019 (ABSENT - only noted as missing requirement)
predicted_outcome: System requires environmental assessment (noise, species impact, fire risk); applicant submits noise model, habitat survey, mitigation plan; CAA assesses against thresholds (residential <70dB, nature reserve <60dB); if exceeds, mitigation required before trial proceeds
realistic_alternative: Environmental assessment is mentioned in regulatory framework but not required by system; trial proceeds without formal environmental review; environmental impacts are discovered post-hoc by community/regulators; trial is challenged on environmental grounds
absurd_alternative: Environmental assessment is conducted but results are not linked to trial authorization; trial is approved regardless of environmental impact; post-incident, environmental damage is discovered; liability falls on CAA for not considering impact
reasoning: REQ019 is completely absent from user stories (only noted as missing). Environmental impact (CAP 722, environmental law) is regulatory background but not specified. This is a significant gap. Two developers might: (1) implement full environmental gate (assessment blocks trial if impact unacceptable); (2) implement advisory-only assessment (no blocking); (3) skip assessment entirely (legal risk). Prediction is impossible.
recommendation: CREATE NEW REQ019: 'As a CAA environmental assessor, I want to assess environmental impact so that noise, species, and fire risks are evaluated and acceptable before trial proceeds.' Requirements: (1) Applicant must submit environmental documentation before gate approval: (a) noise impact model (flight paths, altitude profiles, weather scenarios) with predicted dB levels at sensitive receptors; (b) habitat survey (endangered species breeding seasons, migration patterns) if applicable; (c) battery fire risk mitigation plan (ground handling, charging, fire suppression). (2) CAA environmental assessor reviews documentation against thresholds: residential area <70dB, nature reserve <60dB, species impact risk <acceptable level. (3) If within thresholds, assessment is approved. (4) If exceeds thresholds, applicant must propose mitigation (route change, time restriction, noise barriers); reassess; if still exceeds, trial is rejected on environmental grounds. (5) Environmental assessment is documented in case file. Define environmental thresholds and assessment criteria.'

finding_title: REQ020 (absent): Post-trial close-out and evidence archival outcome completely undefined
scenario: GIVEN trial execution is complete WHEN authorization must transition from active to closed state THEN [outcome undefined: what triggers close-out? what is archive procedure? retention policy?]
source_req: REQ020 (ABSENT - only noted as missing requirement)
predicted_outcome: System implements close-out gate: applicant submits post-trial report (flights, incidents, lessons learned); all evidence is archived immutably (7-year retention minimum); authorization is formally CLOSED; case transitions to archive status
realistic_alternative: Trial completes; case status drifts (active or closed unclear); evidence is stored but retention policy is informal; lessons-learned report is not required; close-out is ad hoc rather than systematic
absurd_alternative: Trial completes; authorization remains active indefinitely; applicant can request new flights under expired authorization; evidence is not archived or is overwritten with new data; post-incident investigation cannot access trial data because retention failed
reasoning: REQ020 is completely absent from user stories (only noted as missing). Post-trial closure and evidence preservation are important for institutional learning and regulatory audit trail. Without explicit specification, close-out is undefined. Two developers might: (1) implement mandatory close-out gate (trial cannot be archived without completion); (2) allow drifting status (trial remains active indefinitely); (3) auto-archive after timeout. Prediction is impossible.
recommendation: CREATE NEW REQ020: 'As a CAA programme manager, I want to close-out trial authorization so that lessons are captured and evidence is preserved for institutional learning.' Requirements: (1) Trial close-out is triggered upon: (a) completion of trial duration (license expiration), or (b) early termination decision by CAA. (2) Applicant must submit post-trial report (within 30 days): flights completed, incidents, lessons learned, unexpected behaviors, design improvements for future trials. (3) CAA case officer reviews close-out package; if complete, authorizes close-out. (4) All trial evidence (flight logs, incident reports, sensor data, assessment records) is transferred to immutable archive with 7-year minimum retention (per aviation accident investigation rules). (5) Authorization status is set to CLOSED; no new flights are permitted under closed authorization. (6) Case file is sealed and moved to historical archive; public access is restricted per privacy/security classification. Define close-out checklist and archive retention policy.'

finding_title: REQ007: DAA low confidence scenario outcome undefined - what happens when detection confidence is low
scenario: GIVEN DAA system detects potential intruder with LOW confidence (sensor ambiguity, intermittent track) WHEN intruder confidence score is <70% THEN [outcome undefined: escalate as threat? dismiss? escalate to crew?]
predicted_outcome: DAA applies confidence threshold: if intruder confidence >80%, treat as definite threat and execute avoidance; if 60-80%, alert crew and allow crew to decide (escalate, dismiss, or override); if <60%, log detection but do not escalate or maneuver
realistic_alternative: DAA treats all detections equally regardless of confidence; all detected targets trigger equivalent avoidance maneuvers; crew is overwhelmed with false positive alerts
absurd_alternative: DAA only acts on high-confidence targets; low-confidence potential collisions are ignored; if low-confidence target becomes high-confidence mid-maneuver (suddenly clear track), avoidance is late; collision risk is unmitigated
reasoning: Real-world sensor systems (radar, vision) produce detections with varying confidence scores. REQ007 requires encounters 'are resolved predictably' but does not address confidence-weighted decision logic. A developer must hardcode thresholds for treating ambiguous detections. Without specification, two developers might implement opposite strategies: fail-safe (low-confidence treated as threat) or fail-open (low-confidence ignored). Prediction is impossible.
recommendation: Add to REQ007: 'DAA system shall apply confidence-weighted response to intruder detections. (1) All detections are assigned confidence score (0-100%) based on sensor agreement and track stability. (2) Confidence >85%: definite threat, execute autonomous avoidance immediately. (3) Confidence 70-85%: likely threat, alert crew with 'CAUTION: Possible intruder [confidence]%'; crew has 5-second window to confirm/dismiss; if crew does not respond, escalate to autonomous avoidance. (4) Confidence <70%: ambiguous detection, log to black box and display to crew as 'Possible target' advisory; no maneuver unless crew selects avoidance. (5) If ambiguous detection is confirmed post-maneuver, high-confidence follow-up actions are enabled. Define confidence scoring method and thresholds.'

finding_title: REQ005-REQ011: Precedence undefined between geofence enforcement and flight termination
scenario: GIVEN swarm aircraft approaches geofence boundary AND simultaneously detects off-nominal state (e.g., structural failure) WHEN both geofence enforcement and flight termination are triggered THEN [outcome undefined: which executes first? can both execute?]
predicted_outcome: Precedence is defined: geofence enforcement (soft-stop, containment) executes first to prevent boundary escape; if containment fails within 10 seconds, escalate to independent termination system. Independent termination is ultimate fallback (last resort).
realistic_alternative: Both systems execute simultaneously; dual motor cutoff occurs; aircraft enters uncontrolled descent; independent termination redundancy is lost because both primary and backup systems fired together
absurd_alternative: Flight termination takes precedence over geofence enforcement; aircraft outside boundary is immediately terminated rather than contained; boundary breach is tolerated because termination occurred; regulatory gap emerges (containment was required but overridden)
reasoning: REQ005 (containment enforcement) and REQ011 (flight termination) are overlapping safety mechanisms. Both are triggered by off-nominal conditions. But the interaction/precedence between them is undefined. If both trigger simultaneously, which takes priority? Is there an escalation sequence? Two developers might implement: (1) containment first, then escalate; (2) termination as immediate backup; (3) parallel execution (both firing simultaneously). Prediction of safety behavior is impossible without explicit precedence.
recommendation: Add requirement: 'Safety system precedence and escalation shall be defined explicitly. (1) When multiple off-nominal triggers are detected (geofence breach AND structural failure detected), safety system applies escalation sequence: (a) LEVEL 1: Containment enforcement (soft-stop, hold position) attempts to prevent boundary breach; (b) if containment is ineffective (aircraft still escaping after soft-stop), escalate within 10 seconds; (c) LEVEL 2: Independent termination system activates (motors cut, controlled descent). (2) Crew is alerted at each escalation level with reason code. (3) Precedence is always: prevent boundary escape first, then mitigate structural hazard. Define escalation timings and sensor-confirmation logic.'
